children.name: Mimus
children.description: Typical mockingbirds — medium-sized, long-tailed passerines with grey upperparts and pale underparts. Renowned for their extraordinary vocal abilities, mockingbirds can imitate dozens of other bird species, as well as frogs, dogs, and mechanical sounds. They are among the most accomplished avian mimics in the New World. Found from the United States to South America.

children.name: Mimus polyglottos
children.description: The classic mockingbird of North America. Grey above, whitish below, with conspicuous white wing patches and a long tail. Its scientific name means 'many-tongued mimic'. Known to incorporate sounds of over 200 other species into its repertoire. The state bird of five states. Males sing from exposed perches, often at night during a full moon.

children.name: Mimus saturninus
children.description: A widespread South American mockingbird found from Brazil and Bolivia to Argentina. Distinguished by a bold white supercilium ('eyebrow'). Inhabits open country, farmland, and gardens. Known for its varied song and bold behaviour around humans.

children.name: Mimus gilvus
children.description: Found from southern Mexico through Central America to northern Brazil, and across the Caribbean. Very similar to the Northern Mockingbird but slightly smaller. Inhabits open grassy areas with scattered bushes.

children.name: Toxostoma
children.description: Thrashers — long-tailed, long-billed songbirds of dry, open country in North America. They use their curved bills to sweep through leaf litter, searching for insects. Their skulking habits often make them harder to see than to hear — their songs are long, complex, and melodious, often given from exposed perches.

children.name: Toxostoma rufum
children.description: A rich rufous-brown thrasher with heavy streaking below. Found across eastern and central North America. Its song is one of the most varied of any North American bird, with each phrase typically repeated twice. Inhabits dense thickets and brushy edges.

children.name: Toxostoma curvirostre
children.description: A pale grey-brown thrasher of the southwestern United States and Mexico. Named for its distinctly down-curved bill. Inhabits desert scrub and thorn woodland. Its call is a distinctive sharp 'whit-wheet'.

children.name: Dumetella
children.description: The grey catbird — a monotypic genus of medium-sized, slate-grey songbird named for its distinctive mewing call that sounds like a cat. Despite its subdued colouration, it is a gifted mimic, often incorporating the songs of other birds into its own rambling, discontinuous song.

children.name: Dumetella carolinensis
children.description: A sleek slate-grey bird with a distinctive black cap and rich chestnut under-tail coverts. Found across North America, wintering in the southeastern US and the Caribbean. Named for its cat-like 'mew' call. Its song is a long, jumbled series of notes, whistles, and imitations.
